Title: Workers to escalate strike at Inpex's Ichthys LNG sites after talks fail
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Workers will escalate the strike at Inpex's Ichthys LNG facilities in Australia that started last week over a wage dispute, after talks failed to progress with the Japanese operator, the Offshore Alliance union said on Monday.
Ichthys accounts for about 10 per cent of LNG supply from Australia, which is now the world's second-largest LNG exporter following damage to facilities in Qatar due to the Iran war.
The Offshore Alliance, made up of the Maritime Union of Australia and the Australian Workers' Union, said it would ramp up the four-hour stoppages and bans at the facilities which commenced last Wednesday.
"Last night, members endorsed work stoppages escalating to eight hours per day, and we'll be loading up a stack of work bans on Thursday," the union said in a Facebook post.
The union said Inpex, Japan's biggest oil and gas explorer, reneged on terms agreed in a Fair Work Commission-facilitated bargaining that took place on Saturday.
The union said Inpex is seeking an enterprise bargaining agreement with weaker conditions than previously discussed, including reduced remote-work benefits, fewer guaranteed full-time positions, lower pay relative to workers' competency levels, and redundancy terms below industry standards.
Reuters could not immediately contact Inpex for comment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 7.8-magnitude quake off Philippines kills at least one, sparks tsunami alerts
Article:
MANILA: A 7.8-magnitude earthquake that struck the southern Philippines on Monday killed at least one person and collapsed buildings, police said, as the disaster sparked tsunami warnings across the region.
The
offshore quake
hit at a depth of 35 kilometres (22 miles) about 24 kilometres west of Mindanao island's Sarangani province, the United States Geological Survey said.
Videos posted to Facebook showed a shopping centre with a Jollibee fast food restaurant collapsing into rubble in the province's General Santos City, while a building on a local school campus crumpled in another.
"Lord, it has really collapsed! It has really collapsed! The building has really collapsed!" someone can be heard shouting.
Master Sergeant Robert Dagon of the General Santos City police said that "a number of buildings collapsed", including homes.
"Many buildings were affected, but I cannot enumerate them now because we are busy with ongoing rescues," he told AFP, confirming at least one death and four injuries.
The Pacific Tsunami Warning Centre said in a notice that tsunami waves were possible "within the next three hours" along the coasts of the Philippines, Indonesia, Palau, Taiwan and Papua New Guinea.
Japanese authorities separately
issued a tsunami advisory
for swathes of its Pacific coast, projecting waves of up to one metre (three feet) to hit different regions from 11.30am local time (0230 GMT).
Indonesia ordered a tsunami evacuation of northern areas following the quake.
In Mindanao's Davao City, a local disaster official said only that authorities were monitoring the situation and would post updates on social media.
Earthquakes are a near-daily occurrence in the Philippines, which is situated on the Pacific "Ring of Fire", an arc of intense seismic activity stretching from Japan through Southeast Asia and across the Pacific basin.
Eastern Mindanao was rocked by a pair of earthquakes of 7.4 and 6.7 magnitude in October that killed at least eight people.
These followed a magnitude 6.9 quake days earlier that killed 76 people and destroyed or damaged 72,000 houses in Cebu province in central Philippines, according to government figures.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Seven OPEC+ countries to raise oil output in July
Article:
VIENNA: The Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) and its allies, known as OPEC+, on Sunday decided to increase oil output by 188,000 barrels per day in July, according to an OPEC statement, reported Xinhua.
The decision was announced following a virtual meeting attended by Saudi Arabia, Russia, Iraq, Kuwait, Kazakhstan, Algeria and Oman, which reviewed global market conditions and outlook.
Such move marks the fourth consecutive month for the OPEC+ to ramp up its production, in spite of the exit of the United Arab Emirates from the OPEC and the wider OPEC+ alliance on May 1.
The reason of the upward production adjustment by the seven OPEC+ countries was the "collective commitment to support oil market stability", the statement said.
According to the statement, the seven countries would retain full flexibility to adjust their production depending on evolving market conditions.
They are scheduled to meet again on July 5 to make further decisions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mindanao quake felt in Sabah, MetMalaysia monitoring situation
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tremors were felt in Tawau, Semporna and surrounding areas following a strong offshore earthquake that struck Mindanao, Philippines, at 7.37am today.
The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) confirmed it is actively monitoring the situation and assessing any potential impact from the quake, which registered a magnitude between 7.8 and 7.9.
The
epicentre was located
about 97km south of Koronadal City at a depth of around 50km.
MetMalaysia also urged members of the public who experienced shaking to submit feedback through its official post-earthquake survey form (https://forms.gle/XpZe1TXrfTGS8ch9A) to support ongoing assessment efforts.
Officials stressed that no tsunami warning has been issued for Malaysia, though regional alerts remain in place.
In Indonesia, the Meteorology, Climatology and Geophysics Agency (BMKG) is also tracking the seismic event closely, with early reports indicating tremors may have been felt in parts of eastern Indonesia, including Sulawesi and nearby regions.
BMKG said it is evaluating the potential tsunami impact along Indonesia's eastern coastline as a precautionary measure.
The Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology (Phivolcs) issued tsunami warnings and advisories shortly after the quake, cautioning of waves potentially exceeding one metre in some coastal areas.
Initial reports from Sarangani indicated structural damage, including cracks in a police building, while several residents were reported to have fainted during the shaking.
Japan also issued a tsunami advisory for parts of its Pacific coastline as a precaution, following the offshore quake and its potential to generate distant wave activity across the region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Iskandar Affan was excited about the new car' - Aunt recalls final moments before crash
Article:
ALOR STAR: A 7-year-old boy was so excited to ride in his uncle's newly purchased Proton X50 that he rushed out of his house to join five family members before all six perished in a horrific crash yesterday.
Norzamilah Ghazali, who is the aunt of Iskandar Affan Ibrahim, said the child was having a meal when he heard the sports utility vehicle (SUV) arrive at his father Ibrahim Ghazali's house in Taman Bertam, Penang.
"Adik looked so excited because he wanted to follow his grandmother and uncles in the newly bought Proton X50.
"He was eating at the time, but the moment he heard them arrive, he washed his hands and rushed out," she said when met at the Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital forensic department grounds here last night.
The 3.50pm accident involving the SUV and a lorry carrying soil at Kampung Baru Pinang Tunggal in Sungai Petani killed six family members.
Those killed at the scene were Iskandar's grandmother Nora Mhd Husin, 55; his uncles Ahmad Shafiq Ahmad Shukri, 29, who was driving, and Ahmad Fahim Ahmad Shukri, 21; his aunt Jamaliah Sannusi, 29, who was Ahmad Shafiq's wife; and their two-month-old baby, Ahmad Mikail Ahmad Shafiq.
Another child of the couple, 3-year-old Aulia Sofea Ahmad Shafiq, was the sole survivor. She sustained severe injuries and is being treated at Sultan Abdul Halim Hospital in Sungai Petani.
Norzamilah said Nora was the mother-in-law of her 39-year-old brother, Ibrahim.
She added that Ibrahim's 8-year-old daughter, who is Iskandar's elder sister, was originally supposed to join her grandmother but changed her mind at the last minute.
"We had just arrived at Ibrahim's house at about 2.30pm because yesterday was my brother's birthday and they were having a small gathering.
"About an hour later, we received a call informing us that they were involved in an accident. At first, I thought only Nora had died, but it turned out six died and only one survived, she said.
Norzamilah said Iskandar appeared the most joyful because he wanted to go for a ride in the new vehicle.
She said the car belonged to Ahmad Fahim, who had just finished his studies and was working at a supermarket in Penang. The vehicle was purchased just yesterday.
"They were believed to be heading to Merbok to visit the grave of Ahmad Shafiq and Ahmad Fahim's late father. We never expected this to happen and that they would leave us forever," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan's economy cools on weak capex in Q1
Article:
TOKYO: Japan's economy lost momentum in the January-March quarter from the previous three months over sluggish capital expenditure, revised gross domestic product (GDP) data showed on Monday, pointing to challenges ahead due to the Middle East conflict.
The GDP figures released by the Cabinet Office showed the economy expanded an annualised 1.8 per cent in the first quarter, compared with the initially estimated 2.1 per cent. Economists' median forecast was for 1.3 per cent growth.
Without annualisation, GDP grew 0.5 per cent, slightly better than the median forecast for a 0.3 per cent expansion and matching the preliminary figure.
Private consumption, which accounts for more than half of Japan's economy, increased 0.3 per cent, also matching the initial data.
Businesses' capital expenditure shrank 0.7 per cent in the first quarter, revised down from the initial estimate for a 0.3 per cent rise and compared with an estimated 0.9 per cent drop.
External demand, or exports minus imports, added 0.3 percentage point to GDP, unchanged from the preliminary data. Domestic demand contributed 0.2 of a percentage point, also matching the initial figure.
Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's government on Wednesday finalised a US$19 billion supplementary budget for this fiscal year to cushion the impact of rising energy costs on households from the Middle East crisis.
U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran in late February and Tehran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which normally carries a fifth of global oil and gas, have sent prices soaring and raised fears of a major disruption to energy flows.
Japan's heavy reliance on Middle Eastern oil leaves it acutely exposed. Rising fuel costs are stoking inflation, eroding household purchasing power and tightening corporate margins, heightening the risk of a severe economic downturn if disruptions persist.
The Bank of Japan will hold a two-day policy meeting next week. Sources told Reuters the bank is expected to raise interest rates this month unless a sharp escalation in the conflict roils markets.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK firms pause hiring as Iran war stings
Article:
LONDON: Britain's jobs market cooled rapidly in May after employers put the brakes on permanent hiring after the Iran war heightened cost pressures and uncertainty about the economic outlook, an industry survey showed on Monday.
The monthly Report on Jobs from accountants KPMG and the Recruitment and Employment Confederation, a trade body, showed permanent job placements fell at the fastest pace since July 2025. Placements have fallen for 44 months in a row, the longest period of contraction since the survey began in 1997.
"Ongoing global and domestic uncertainty is making businesses more cautious, and that is increasingly reflected in hiring decisions. While some employers are turning to temporary contracts to retain flexibility, many permanent hiring plans are being delayed or put on hold," Jon Holt, KPMG's group chief executive, said.
REC and KPMG also said:
* Permanent staff placements fell to 44.1 in May, down sharply from 47.5 in April
* Firms ramped up hiring of temporary staff, with the index rising to 52.2 from 50.4, expanding at the quickest pace since April 2023, as firms opted for short-term staff due to uncertainty about the Iran war
* The availability of candidates for permanent and temporary roles also rose faster than in April
* Vacancies dropped at the sharpest pace since February, driven by a sharper fall in vacancies for permanent roles
* The survey was based on responses from a panel of around 400 recruitment agencies between May 12 and May 22
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tiny homes see tiny boost for Los Angeles homeless as World Cup nears
Article:
LOS ANGELES: For the past few months, Michael Gilpin has slept in a small, prefabricated house he moved into when he got off the streets of Los Angeles as part of a major drive to reduce homelessness.
The single-room unit is a long way from perfect – he says it has the air of a "jail cell" – but it's a huge improvement on sleeping in his car.
"It's better than the streets, hands down," the 44-year-old told AFP of his 65-square-foot home (six-square-meter), which he shares with another man. "I don't have to deal with cockroaches."
Gilpin is one of a few thousand people who have benefited from hundreds of millions of dollars spent to find people a place to live in Los Angeles.
America's second biggest city will host eight games in the World Cup this summer, but faces even bigger global scrutiny in 2028 when the Olympics come to town, bringing athletes and fans from all over the world.
Visitors to Los Angeles are frequently struck by the staggering levels of homelessness in a city that also has pockets of astonishing wealth.
Squalid tent encampments pack sidewalks all over the city, where the newly homeless and the down-on-their-luck live cheek-by-jowl with drug addicts and people suffering from chronic – and untreated – mental health problems.
For the three years of her term, Democratic Mayor Karen Bass has made tackling this blight a priority, ramping up initiatives to fund thousands of beds, whether in hotels or in so-called "tiny homes" like the one Gilpin occupies.
The effort is beginning to bear fruit: the clusters of tents and shopping carts that once clogged the boulevards of Hollywood and Venice Beach are less numerous.
The latest census, released last year, showed a 17.5 percent drop in the number of people living on the streets over a two-year period - the most sustained fall since the city began counting its homeless just 20 years ago.
- 'Not enough' -
Yet, the scale of the problem remains immense. Los Angeles County, which includes the metropolis's suburbs, has an official tally of 72,000 unhoused people, 47,000 of whom sleep on the street.
In the San Fernando Valley, north of the city, activist Armando Covarrubias manages the crisis as best he can during his daily rounds, distributing bottles of water, snacks, and instant soup.
"Unfortunately, there's not enough beds, not enough shelters," says Covarrubias, who works for an organization called Hope The Mission.
In his area, the number of homeless people is "four or five times bigger than the beds."
When city authorities dismantled an encampment along the side of a railway line last month, he couldn't find housing for everyone.
As a result, about a dozen tents have already sprung up again.
Amidst the tarps and makeshift barbecue, a lady named Maggie said she is hoping to find somewhere permanent after 10 years on the street.
Maggie, who is in her 40s and did not want to give her full name, said she is on a waiting list.
"I've been waiting three months for them to help me," she said.
Even for those who secure a spot in a shelter, success is far from guaranteed.
The facilities impose rules that are sometimes hard to accept, such as a ban on visitors.
The mayor's flagship program – a short-term shelter system using tiny homes and a network of hotels – has drawn criticism.
Despite the $300 million outlay, it has not been a total success: figures show that by the end of 2025, it had got 5,800 people into some kind of accommodation, but 40 percent of them eventually returned to the streets.
- Housing crisis -
Fundamentally, the city remains in the grips of a housing crisis affecting all of California.
Experts say the Golden State is simply not building enough affordable housing - or indeed, enough housing in general.
That throttled supply means property prices – and therefore rents – make up an unaffordable proportion of many people's income.
A small adjustment in wages can have catastrophic consequences.
That's what happened to maintenance worker Michael Reyes after he suffered a workplace accident and found his monthly benefits were no longer enough to cover his living expenses and rent in a city where a studio apartment averages US$1,800 a month.
"Our cost of living is going up, but not our income," he told AFP. "There's something wrong there."
Reyes is now living in a tiny home after spending a year sleeping in the back of his car.
At 59, he is disillusioned and doubts that Los Angeles will sustain its efforts beyond the Olympic Games.
"They're just doing it for tourists," he says.
"'Oh let's clean up Hollywood.' But it's never going to change."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Five injured in stabbing at New York's Penn Station
Article:
NEW YORK: Five people were wounded Sunday in a stabbing at New York's Penn Station, according to the city fire department, which said all victims were transported to hospital and the suspect was detained.
The victims were all civilians, including one person with serious injuries, two with moderate injuries and two with minor injuries, the New York Fire Department said in a brief statement.
"Perp in custody," it said. "Operations ongoing."
Circumstances of the stabbing were not immediately clear.
The incident occurred at one of the nation's busiest rail and subway transportation centres as the city gears up to host two major sporting events in the coming week: basketball's NBA Finals and football's World Cup tournament.
Madison Square Garden, located directly above Penn Station in downtown Manhattan, will host games three and four of the NBA Finals on Monday and Wednesday between the New York Knicks and the San Antonio Spurs.
MetLife stadium, outside the city in neighbouring New Jersey state, will host its first match of the World Cup on Saturday.
US President Donald Trump is scheduled to attend Monday's NBA game at Madison Square Garden, saying recently that he accepted an invitation from Knicks owner James Dolan.
Security has been enhanced in the city ahead of the two events.
New York's official emergency notification system did not describe the incident but said people should avoid the area and "expect traffic delays, road closures, mass transit disruptions & emergency personnel near Penn Station."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: 'Unsung heroes': Praise pours in for SAR teams in Gunung Batu Putih rescue
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The dedication and sacrifice of rescue teams and volunteers involved in the search-and-rescue (SAR) operation for missing hiker Jaslinda Saludin, 49, in Gunung Batu Putih, Perak, have drawn praise online, with social media users hailing their efforts as heroic and selfless.
Joint coordinator of the Malim Gunung SAR team, Muzafar Mohamad, who
shared his experience in a Facebook
post, said the operation, which lasted several days across dense forest and hilly terrain, involved significant risks, with some rescuers nearly losing their lives during the mission.
He said the operation was not only physically demanding but also mentally and emotionally taxing for all those involved.
He said several volunteers faced dangerous situations while carrying out search efforts based on plans coordinated at the incident control post.
"To the colleagues who nearly lost their lives while executing search missions based on control post coordination, I salute all of you," he said in the post.
He said many volunteers sacrificed their Hari Raya Aidiladha celebrations and time with family to take part in the operation.
"We all made sacrifices during Aidiladha. Not only the agencies on duty, but also the mountain guide volunteers who gave up time with their families to join the mission."
Muzafar said many volunteers also used their own funds to cover travel expenses and operational needs, while collectively preparing food and essential supplies throughout the mission.
He added that most of those involved did not personally know Jaslinda or her family, but still came forward out of humanitarian concern and compassion.
"Everyone who came did so with sincerity and a humanitarian spirit. May all contributions made during the SAR efforts be accepted," he said.
He also said that prayers from Jaslinda's family, friends and the public, along with the call to prayer echoing repeatedly in the jungle, created deeply emotional moments during the operation.
In comment sections, many social media users described the rescuers as "unsung heroes", while others expressed gratitude and respect for their willingness to risk their safety in difficult terrain.
"Thank you, unsung heroes. To all members, government agencies and civilian SAR teams involved. Even though the public may not see it now, your contributions are deeply appreciated," wrote user Lordman Kerol.
"Thank you for the blood and sweat shed in the name of humanity. May Allah accept all your good deeds. Rest well, heroes," said Umi Zainurrina.
"Alhamdulillah… thank you to all the brothers, the SAR team and everyone involved. May Allah reward all your efforts in the search for Kak Jaslinda, and may she recover quickly," commented Anggun Twissterberry.
Jaslinda was
found safe near Kampung Lubuk Gaharu
in Pos Musoh, Tapah, yesterday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'I felt I was going to lose him': Mother's premonition before crash killed son, five relatives
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: A mother's premonition of losing her son came true in the most devastating way when her seven-year-old was killed alongside five family members on Sunday.
Atikah, the mother of Iskandar Affan Ibrahim, said she felt an unexplainable sense of impending loss in the days leading up to the horrific road crash.
"As a mother, I just had this feeling that something was not right. It was as though I was going to lose him," she said when met after her son's burial this morning.
Arikah said she also noticed subtle changes in Iskandar's behaviour before the incident, as if he was preparing to go somewhere.
She described Iskandar as an affectionate, pampered child with a strong fascination for vehicles.
"He was very manja. He loved cars and would get very excited whenever he saw them," she said.
The family's grief was compounded by memories of Iskandar's final hours, with his aunt, Nor Zamilah Ghazali, noting the boy appeared unusually cheerful before leaving home.
"He seemed very excited. My eldest child was feeding him earlier and noticed he was eating more than usual.
"My child told me, Mak, adik is eating a lot today. Usually, he only ate a little because he preferred watching television or playing. But today, he finished his meal properly," she said.
Nor Zamilah said the family was initially told only about the death of Iskandar's grandmother before rushing to the scene.
"When we arrived, we already sensed something was wrong. We asked Fire and Rescue Department personnel and were told only Aulia had survived. The others had died at the scene," she said.
Nor Zamilah described the family as exceptionally close-knit.
"We did everything together. Even if someone did not have enough money, the others would help. That was how our father raised us. We always travelled and spent time together as a family," she said.
Meanwhile, Iskandar's father, Ibrahim Ghazali, appealed to the public to continue praying for the victims.
"Please pray that my son and the others are at peace there," he said briefly, while thanking Malaysians for their support during the family's darkest hour.
Iskandar was among six family members who perished in the tragic accident in Pinang Tunggal, near Sungai Petani, on Sunday afternoon.
Besides Iskandar, those killed were Ahmad Syafiq Ahmad Shukri, 29; Ahmad Fahim Ahmad Shukri, 21; Jamaliah Sannusi, 29; Nora Mohd Husin, 55; and two-month-old Ahmad Mikail Ahmad Syafiq.
The sole survivor, three-year-old Aulia Sofea Ahmad Syafiq, sustained serious facial injuries.
Funeral arrangements for the remaining victims are expected to be carried out in Merbok, Kedah, today.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: First of six family members killed in tragic Sungai Petani crash laid to rest
Article:
KEPALA BATAS: Seven-year-old Iskandar Affan Ibrahim, who was among six victims killed in a horrific road accident yesterday, was laid to rest early today.
The Year One pupil is the first of the victims to be buried.
A sombre mood enveloped his grandparents' home in Penaga earlier as family members, relatives, friends, and neighbours gathered to pay their final respects.
His remains were brought there just after 1am from the Forensic Department of Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital in Alor Star, Kedah.
Iskandar's remains were later brought to Masjid Permatang Kuang Penaga for funeral prayers.
Nearly 100 people were present to offer condolences and comfort to the grieving family members.
Following the prayers, the body was taken to the cemetery next to the mosque, where he was laid to rest at 2.30am in the presence of mourners.
Many in attendance were visibly emotional as the funeral procession made its way from the mosque to the burial ground.
His father, Ibrahim Ghazali, was a picture of grief throughout.
Iskandar was among six family members who perished in a tragic accident in Pinang Tunggal, near Sungai Petani, yesterday afternoon.
The 3.50pm crash involved a sport utility vehicle (SUV) carrying seven family members, including a policeman, and a lorry carrying soil.
The family was heading towards Sungai Petani on their way to Merbok to visit the graves of other family members, while the lorry, driven by a 49-year-old man, was heading towards Tikam Batu.
The accident
killed six of the family members,
while a three-year-old girl sustained severe facial injuries.
The SUV had just left the showroom earlier that day.
Besides Iskandar, those killed were Ahmad Syafiq Ahmad Shukri, 29; Ahmad Fahim Ahmad Shukri, 21; Jamaliah Sannusi, 29; Nora Mohd Husin, 55; and two-month-old Ahmad Mikail Ahmad Syafiq.
The injured victim was identified as Aulia Sofea Ahmad Syafiq, 3.
Funeral arrangements for the remaining victims are expected to be carried out later this morning in Merbok, Kedah.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil prices rise more than $2 on Israel strikes on Lebanon
Article:
BEIJING: Oil prices were up more than US$2 a barrel on Monday after Israel on Sunday launched renewed strikes on Lebanon despite a truce between the two countries, eroding hopes for an end to the wider war and a restart to crude flows through the Strait of Hormuz.
U.S. crude futures were up US$2.10, or 2.32 per cent, at US$92.64 per barrel as of 0013 GMT, while Brent crude futures rose US$2.33, or 2.5 per cent, to US$95.42 a barrel.
That erased most of the losses from Friday, when prices had fallen on mounting hopes of a deescalation in the U.S.-Iran conflict, which started with U.S. and Israel strikes on Iran in February.
The latest strikes appeared to present yet another barrier to a U.S.-Iran peace deal and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a key conduit for global oil and gas flows. Iran has made a ceasefire with Lebanon a condition for a peace deal with Washington.
Iran retaliated for the Beirut strikes on its ally Hezbollah by launching missiles at Israel. U.S. President Donald Trump said he would tell Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu not to retaliate at Iran.
Israel had invaded Lebanon in March after Iran-backed Hezbollah fired rockets and drones across the border. Lebanon and Israel said on June 3 that they had agreed to a ceasefire following negotiations in Washington.
The two countries had previously agreed to a cessation of hostilities in April but violence continued.
The wider war has been on pause since the U.S. and Israel halted their attacks on Iran in early April, but with Tehran continuing to block most shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
Amid the resulting supply crisis, OPEC+ on Sunday agreed its fourth increase in oil output in four months. But analysts said the decision would have little impact since most OPEC+ members could not meet their output targets because of the Hormuz closure or, in the case of Russia, infrastructure attacks that have eroded its production capacity.
"In the current market, the physical impact of such a decision would be close to zero," Rystad Energy head of geopolitical analysis Jorge Leon said in a note. (Reporting by Colleen Howe; Editing by Edmund Klamann and Christopher Cushing)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asia markets tumble as tech rout deepens
Article:
SINGAPORE: Asian markets plunged on Monday as investors slammed the brakes on the red-hot AI rally, while Israeli strikes on Beirut sent oil prices and the dollar higher.
An 8 per cent drop for South Korea's chip-heavy KOSPI benchmark triggered a 20-minute trading halt and has it down almost 17 per cent from last week's record high.
Japan's Nikkei fell 3.5 per cent in early trade, though U.S. S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 futures made small gains.
The Nasdaq had dropped 4.2 per cent on Friday, with selling concentrated in semiconductor stocks after a hot jobs report ramped up expectations for Federal Reserve interest rate hikes, putting the brakes on what has been a sparkling AI-led rally.
Two-year Treasury yields rose more than 11 basis points on Friday and benchmark 10-year Treasury futures were about five ticks lower early on Monday morning in Asia.
"The AI-drives-everything narrative frayed last week," said Bob Savage, head of markets macro strategy at BNY.
"Whether this is a healthy pause in the nine-week equity rally or a top remains the key question. The IPO focus on SpaceX and Anthropic is part of the pause – whether to make room for the new market cap or to rethink value."
INFLATION AND ECB AHEAD
The week ahead is headlined by the giant SpaceX listing, expected to price on Thursday and trade on Friday, but will also have inflation in focus with U.S. consumer price data due on Wednesday and central bank meetings in Canada and Europe.
Last week, bitcoin notched its heaviest weekly drop since the collapse of crypto exchange FTX in late 2022, falling about 16 per cent. It was hovering just shy of US$63,000 on Monday.
SpaceX's debut is expected to be followed by other mega IPOs in the coming months from Anthropic and OpenAI, raising so much money that brokers are nervous it could draw down other assets.
The Middle East situation also remains delicate, and Brent crude futures were up about 2.6 per cent to US$95.45 a barrel on Monday morning after an Israeli attack on Beirut prompted Iran to direct a salvo of missiles at Israeli targets.
OPEC+ agreed on Sunday to the fourth increase in its oil output targets in as many months.
In currency trade the dollar was firm and holding above 160 yen and pushed the Australian dollar to US$0.7038. The euro hovered at US$1.1518.
(Reporting by Tom Westbrook; Editing by Aurora Ellis and Shri Navaratnam)
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Perkeso flagged Daya Kerjaya 2.0 irregularities before MACC probe
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Human Resources Ministry has clarified that suspected irregularities involving claims under the Daya Kerjaya 2.0 Programme were first detected by the Social Security Organisation's (Perkeso) internal anti-fraud mechanisms before the case was referred to the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC).
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri Ramanan Ramakrishnan said the ministry fully supports MACC's ongoing investigation and remains committed to ensuring government assistance programmes are administered with the highest standards of integrity, accountability and good governance.
"Perkeso's Anti-Fraud, Ethics and Integrity (AFEI) Division identified suspicious patterns and potential irregularities through internal data analysis conducted in late 2024.
"Following the initial detection, AFEI launched intelligence-gathering efforts, surveillance and comprehensive investigations to verify the findings and determine the extent of the suspected abuse involving claims under the employment incentive programme," Ramanan said in a statement today.
He said investigations continued throughout 2025, during which evidence was collected, records scrutinised and the alleged modus operandi established.
"The findings were subsequently presented to Perkeso's group chief executive officer, who directed that the matter be referred to MACC for further action.
"Perkeso formally handed over the case to MACC's Perak office in August 2025 before the investigation was later escalated to MACC headquarters in Putrajaya as the scope of the case expanded."
Ramanan said Perkeso had provided full cooperation throughout the investigation, including furnishing all relevant documents, records, information and evidence required by the authorities.
The ministry said the case demonstrated that Perkeso's governance, compliance, and anti-fraud mechanisms were functioning effectively, noting that the suspected misconduct was uncovered through the agency's internal controls before being referred to the enforcement authorities.
"The fact that the matter was initially detected through Perkeso's internal controls, thoroughly investigated by its AFEI Division and subsequently referred to MACC reflects the organisation's unwavering commitment to safeguarding public funds and ensuring that government assistance reaches its intended beneficiaries," the statement read.
Ramanan also commended the professionalism and diligence of AFEI officers involved in detecting, investigating and reporting the suspected abuse.
He stressed that the Daya Kerjaya 2.0 Programme was introduced to create employment opportunities and support vulnerable groups, including persons with disabilities, former prisoners, senior citizens and other jobseekers seeking to re-enter the workforce.
He described any abuse of government incentives as a serious matter that undermines public trust and deprives deserving beneficiaries of assistance intended for them.
"The ministry fully supports firm action against any individual or entity found to have misused public funds or submitted fraudulent claims, in accordance with the law," it said.
Ramanan added that the ministry would continue working closely with MACC and other enforcement agencies while strengthening governance frameworks, verification processes and monitoring mechanisms to prevent leakages and abuse in employment-related incentive programmes.
Yesterday, MACC Chief Commissioner Datuk Seri Abd Halim Aman said the commission had
uncovered irregularities
involving the programme, with false claims amounting to RM9.01 million.
He had said investigations showed that 143 companies involving 320 workers nationwide were linked to the alleged misuse of government funds in 2024 and 2025.
MACC investigators had also detected cases involving workers who did not meet the minimum six-month employment requirement but received the full incentive payment, as well as the use of forged documents to obtain the payments.
Another 1,638 companies are suspected of engaging in similar activities involving about RM45 million, although those cases remain at a preliminary stage of investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan issues tsunami advisory after Philippines quake
Article:
TOKYO: Japanese authorities issued a tsunami advisory on Monday along swathes of its Pacific coast after a
major earthquake
off the Philippines.
The Japan Meteorological Agency said that tsunamis of up to one metre were projected to hit different regions from 11.30am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Magnitude 7.8 quake hits Mindanao, tsunami warning issued
Article:
MINDANAO: Tsunami warnings were issued after an earthquake of magnitude 7.8 struck off Mindanao in the southern Philippines on Monday, the German Research Centre for Geosciences said.
The quake struck at a depth of 10km, GFZ said. The geophysics agencies of the Philippines and neighbouring Indonesia issued tsunami warnings. There were no immediate reports of major damage in either country.
GFZ had earlier pegged the earthquake at 8.2. The US Tsunami Warning System issued a tsunami threat to the earthquake.
Phivolcs, the Philippine agency, said the quake was magnitude 7.0 and warned of damage and tsunami waves above one metre, which could continue for several hours.
Indonesia's BMKG put the quake at a 7.7 magnitude.
Benjie Ancheta, police chief of Alabel town in Sarangani in the Philippines, said the police building had some cracks immediately after the quake, which occurred during their flag-raising ceremony.
Ancheta said there were no immediate reports of casualties, but some people fainted following the strong tremor.
"This is the strongest earthquake we've experienced," Ancheta told Reuters by phone.
Witnesses in Indonesia's northern city of Manado said the quake felt very strong.
The Philippines and Indonesia are tectonically complex parts of ​the "Pacific Ring of Fire", a seismically active belt stretching from South America to the Russian Far East. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Peru exit polls show Fujimori ahead in ultra-tight presidential runoff
Article:
LIMA: Voting stations closed Sunday in Peru's presidential election, with exit polls showing four-time candidate Keiko Fujimori with the slenderest of leads over her leftist rival.
Pollsters at Ipsos and other firms said Fujimori led Roberto Sanchez by about one per centage point – 50.7 per cent versus 49.3 per cent – a statistical tie.
Crime and anger over chronic political instability dominated the race to choose the country's ninth leader in a decade.
Many voters are just hoping the election can end years of political chaos that has seen a string of presidents jailed, deposed and impeached.
"We have to choose between the 'lesser evil'," said disgruntled 23-year-old voter Renzo Masa.
Fujimori, 51, is hoping to ride a wave of support for right-wing candidates who have won recent elections in Bolivia, Chile and Ecuador with a tough-on-crime message.
She appeals to the legacy of her late father, Alberto Fujimori, who stabilized the economy and crushed a Maoist insurgency, but was convicted of corruption and crimes against humanity.
Sanchez, a 57-year-old former psychologist, surged late in the race to reach the runoff, thanks to support in poorer rural areas.
He has moderated his early calls for "radical change" and told AFP he wants a "respectful" relationship with US President Donald Trump.
April's first round was marred by logistical problems and a vote count that took weeks to complete, deepening distrust in Peru's creaking institutions.
From the Andes to the Amazon, around 27 million Peruvians can elect their president and voting is compulsory.
"Today is very important. Every vote counts," Fujimori said.
She has promised to militarise prisons, expel migrants and tackle crime with the "same force" used by her father against insurgents in the 1990s.
On the eve of the election, a judge said Sanchez must stand trial over past financial irregularities in his party, raising claims of interference.
If he wins, he would have presidential immunity, though remain vulnerable to the country's right-leaning legislature – which has ousted several recent presidents.
"I hope the entire process is carried out transparently, that the people's vote is respected," early voter Evelyn Pazos, 43, told AFP.
Sanchez has the backing of former president Pedro Castillo, a schoolteacher jailed after a failed attempt to dissolve Congress in 2022.
Sanchez is rarely seen in public without a broad-brimmed palm straw hat gifted to him by his mentor, whom he plans to pardon.
- 'COMMUNISM' OR 'DICTATORSHIP' -
Neither Sanchez nor Fujimori has a legislative majority. Whoever wins must build alliances to complete their term, according to analyst Jeffrey Radzinsky.
The winner will replace interim president Jose Maria Balcazar from July 28.
Despite political disillusionment, Peruvians' main concern is security, as criminal gangs spread and extortion complaints spiked ninefold in five years.
"They kill, dismember, demand protection money. Enough!" said 58-year-old taxi driver Roberto Lovaton.
The winner will inherit a stable economy, with GDP growth of over three per cent and low inflation.
However, seven out of 10 workers are in the informal economy.
Fujimori supports neoliberal policies, property rights and attracting US investment.
Sanchez has promised wage increases and sought to reassure investors by pledging to maintain an open economy and central bank independence, key to economic stability.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Plantation earnings outlook brightens after soft Q1
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's listed plantation firms post softer earnings in the first quarter (Q1) of 2026 but their outlook should improve in the subsequent quarters.
Analysts said industry players could benefit from firmer crude palm oil (CPO) prices, with potential upside from a possible El Niño weather event in the second half of 2026.
Kenanga Research said a weaker seasonal output during Q1 was compounded by lower CPO prices amid higher-than-expected inventory levels.
Its analyst Khoo Teng Chuan said the sector broadly tracked expectations, although results fell short of market consensus.
Khoo expects earnings to improve ahead as CPO prices recover, although downstream margins are likely to remain under pressure.
The weaker quarter was partly due to slower-than-usual harvests, which coincided with softer selling prices caused by an inventory overhang.
In a typical Q1, seasonal production weakness often supports CPO prices, but the combination of slower demand and higher stocks weighed on margins.
Around 67 per cent of plantation companies tracked by Kenanga Research missed consensus estimates, while 56 per cent met expectations.
The earnings shortfall was largely due to weaker CPO prices, although some companies faced additional pressures, the firm noted.
Property earnings at several planters, including Genting Plantations Bhd and Kuala Lumpur Kepong Bhd, were softer than expected.
PPB Group Bhd's associate Wilmar International saw earnings affected by mark-to-market losses, which Kenanga Research expects could reverse in coming quarters.
Heavy rainfall also affected TSH Resources Bhd's first-quarter harvest more significantly than usual, although production has since improved.
Downstream performance was mixed, with earnings weaker quarter-on-quarter but stronger year-on-year.
Non-plantation contributions improved, supported by land disposal gains, including a RM160 million contribution from Sime Darby Plantation Bhd, which offset slower property development profits.
Looking ahead, Kenanga Research said the sector could benefit from firmer CPO prices, with potential upside from a possible El Niño weather event in the second half of 2026.
The severity of the phenomenon remains uncertain, it added.
The firm maintained CPO price assumptions at RM4,250 per tonne for 2026 and RM4,200 per tonne for 2027, keeping an "overweight" view on the sector.
Among preferred picks, IOI Corp Bhd was highlighted for its earnings strength and limited exposure to Indonesia, while KL Kepong offers sensitivity to CPO prices.
Smaller player TSH Resources provides upstream exposure and long-term growth potential despite its Indonesian operations, Kenanga Research said.
MBSB Reasearch remains "Tactical Positive" on the plantation sector, maintaining its 2026 CPO price target of RM4,300 per tonne despite expectations of rising cost pressures in the second half of the year.
The firm said the sector's Q1 earnings were generally satisfactory, with six of seven companies under its coverage meeting expectations.
Their performance was supported by higher fresh fruit bunch output, steady CPO production and improved oil extraction rates of about 20 per cent, aided by favourable weather conditions.
However, MBSB Research said profitability was weighed down by softer CPO prices, with the average realised price easing 1.0 per cent quarter-on-quarter and 12.4 per cent year-on-year to RM4,139 per tonne.
Malaysia's CPO production rose 11.1 per cent year-on-year to 4.2 million tonnes during the quarter, supporting stronger mill utilisation and sales volumes.
The firm expects production to strengthen further, potentially lifting stockpiles and limiting upside to CPO prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia buffered from global tensions by energy exporter status: Bank of America
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's economy remains relatively resilient despite rising global tensions that have driven up fuel, fertiliser and broader commodity prices, Bank of America Malaysia top executive said.
The bank's country executive Gautam Puntambekar said the impact of recent global disruptions is not unique to Malaysia, but its position as a net energy exporter provides a structural buffer against external shocks.
"From a Malaysia perspective, we have to keep in mind a couple of things. One, it is a net energy exporter, so higher oil prices benefit the national oil company Petronas, which in turn supports government revenues and subsidy mechanisms.
"Fuel subsidies, particularly for RON95, remain a key stabilising factor for consumers. While consumption has come down from 300 litres to 200 litres per person, the fact is that RON95 is still at RM1.99, and that is because the country is in a unique position as a net energy exporter," he told Business Times in a recent interview.
However, Gautam noted that prolonged high oil prices could eventually filter through the economy and raise inflationary pressures.
"If oil prices remain above 100, we will start seeing inflationary pressures," he said, adding that higher energy costs typically take around two months to translate into consumer prices.
He said inflationary spillovers would not be limited to food, but could extend to transport and broader cost structures across the economy.
Despite these risks, Malaysia's macroeconomic indicators remain solid, supported by domestic demand and policy buffers.
He added that inflation stood at 1.4 per cent last year, while first-quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth came in at 5.3 per cent, above official expectations of four-five per cent.
"Not many countries can boast of outperforming their expectations," he said, describing Malaysia's overall position as "in good shape" from a resilience and domestic demand standpoint.
On the outlook for growth amid geopolitical uncertainty, Gautam said visibility remains limited given rapidly evolving global conditions.
He instead pointed to Bank of America's internal research view, which has recently revised Malaysia's full-year GDP forecast to 4.2 per cent, broadly in line with Bank Negara Malaysia's projection range of four to five per cent.
He emphasised that while forecasts remain fluid, Malaysia's underlying economic structure continues to provide stability.
On investor sentiment, Gautam said corporate clients remain broadly resilient, though increasingly cautious in capital deployment decisions.
"We haven't heard anybody being too worried. Multinational companies continue to explore opportunities in Malaysia as part of supply chain diversification strategies," he said.
He said investment decisions are still being made, but at a more measured pace, with extended decision cycles and higher cash buffers at headquarters reflecting global uncertainty.
"It's not decoupling, but it is adding another supply chain to your production lines," he said, describing sentiment as "cautiously optimistic".
He added that foreign direct investment commitments into Malaysia remained strong last year, with execution expected to continue into 2026.
Gautam also highlighted growing interest in regional initiatives such as the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone, where Bank of America is a partner bank promoting the project to North American investors.
On trade policy risks, he said uncertainty around US tariffs remains a concern for exporters due to difficulties in pricing and planning.
He said trade policy uncertainty poses challenges, as without clarity on tariffs, it is difficult to determine final selling prices.
However, he noted that Malaysia's export performance remains robust, particularly in electrical and electronics, suggesting external headwinds have not materially disrupted trade flows.
"Perception is tough, but on the ground realities do not necessarily match the worries that people have," he said.
On corporate financial strategies, Gautam said Bank of America is encouraging clients to prioritise hedging strategies amid currency volatility, stressing that foreign exchange exposure should not be treated as a profit centre.
He said making money on foreign exchange moves is not their primary business.
He added that clients are increasingly focusing on liquidity management, with some shifting funds to offshore or regional headquarters as a precautionary buffer against global volatility.
At the same time, this shift is creating opportunities for more onshore financing activity, particularly as companies recalibrate intercompany funding structures.
Overall, Gautam described Malaysia as a relatively well-positioned economy navigating global uncertainty with policy support, strong domestic demand and sustained investor interest, albeit under a more cautious global investment cycle.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Beyond petroleum, beyond politics
Article:
ALBERTA accounts for roughly four-fifths of Canada's oil production and possesses one of the world's largest proven petroleum reserves.
Yet the province's recurring sovereignty movement demonstrates a reality often overlooked in public debates: resource disputes are rarely about resources alone.
For many observers, Alberta's growing sovereignty movement appears to be about oil.
The province contributes significantly to Canada's economy and has long argued that federal policies crafted in Ottawa do not always reflect Alberta's economic realities.
Rising frustration over environmental regulations, energy policy and fiscal transfers has periodically fuelled calls for greater provincial autonomy and, among some groups, even secession from Canada.
Yet oil is merely the visible surface of a much deeper political question.
At its core, the Alberta debate concerns the same issue confronting many federations across the world: how should power, resources, representation and legitimacy be balanced within a single sovereign state?
This is not a uniquely Canadian challenge.
The United Kingdom has faced it through Scotland and North Sea oil. Spain continues to grapple with it through Catalonia. Belgium has experienced it through recurring tensions between Flanders and Wallonia.
Increasingly, Malaysia encounters similar questions through the evolving relationship between the Federation, Petronas, Sabah and Sarawak.
The common thread is striking.
Resource disputes are rarely about resources alone. They are usually about voice. They are about recognition.
And ultimately, they are about trust.
The Return of the Nation-State
The modern international system was built upon the foundations established by the 1648 Peace of Westphalia, which introduced the principles of territorial sovereignty, political authority and non-interference in domestic affairs.
For centuries, the Westphalian state became the primary unit of international order.
Governments exercised authority within defined borders, represented their populations internationally and maintained the legitimacy necessary to govern diverse societies.
Following the end of the Cold War, many believed this model was gradually losing relevance.
Thinkers such as Francis Fukuyama envisioned a world where liberal democracy had triumphed permanently. Others, such as Kenichi Ohmae, argued that economic integration and globalisation would increasingly diminish the importance of national borders and state sovereignty.
Reality has proven more complicated.
Far from disappearing, questions of identity, sovereignty and political representation have returned with renewed intensity.
Brexit, Scottish nationalism, Catalan separatism, Quebec nationalism and Alberta sovereignty movements all demonstrate that globalisation has not erased local identities. In many cases, it has strengthened them.
The nation-state survived. But so too did regional aspirations.
The challenge today is therefore not choosing between national unity and regional autonomy.
It is finding sustainable ways to reconcile both.
Oil, Federalism and Political Legitimacy
Alberta offers an important lesson.
Many Albertans do not necessarily reject Canada. Rather, they seek a larger role in decisions affecting their economic future. The debate is therefore less about separation than about participation.
This distinction matters.
Strong federations are not sustained solely through constitutional authority or legal arrangements. They depend equally on political legitimacy and institutional trust.
Courts can clarify jurisdiction. They can interpret legislation. They can settle legal disputes. But they cannot fully resolve questions of political confidence.
This insight is particularly relevant beyond Canada.
Across federations, resource-rich regions increasingly seek not merely a larger share of economic benefits but also a greater voice in how strategic assets are governed.
As economic development advances and regional institutions become stronger, demands for consultation, recognition and participation naturally increase.
The lesson is not that federalism is failing.
The lesson is that federalism must continuously evolve.
Successful federations are living political arrangements rather than static constitutional structures.
Malaysia's Statecraft Challenge
Malaysia's own federal journey reflects this reality. The ongoing discussions surrounding Petronas, resource governance and the aspirations of Sabah and Sarawak should not be viewed through the narrow lens of hydrocarbons alone.
Petroleum may be the immediate subject of debate, but the broader issue concerns the future evolution of Malaysian federalism itself.
The central question is whether national strategic coordination and stronger regional participation can coexist within a single coherent federation.
This is particularly important in an increasingly uncertain world.
Malaysia's federal arrangements are not merely constitutional questions.
They are also questions of resource governance, economic resilience and national capacity.
In 2025 alone, Petronas contributed RM32 billion in dividends to the federal government, underscoring the strategic importance of the energy sector to national finances, fiscal stability and long-term economic development.
Decisions concerning resource governance therefore carry implications that extend well beyond hydrocarbons themselves.
Indeed, modern strategic resources are no longer limited to oil and gas. Energy security, critical minerals, digital infrastructure, semiconductors and strategic supply chains have all become essential components of national resilience.
In this environment, institutions that manage and coordinate strategic assets become increasingly important to a country's economic competitiveness and strategic autonomy.
Investors and markets consequently place a premium on clarity. They seek certainty regarding authority, regulation and long-term policy direction.
In an era of geopolitical uncertainty, investors increasingly assess not only economic fundamentals but also the clarity and predictability of federal governance arrangements.
Institutional ambiguity carries costs. Capital can tolerate taxation. It can tolerate regulation. What it struggles to accommodate is uncertainty over authority, overlapping jurisdictions and inconsistent signals regarding who ultimately speaks for the country on matters of strategic importance.
At the same time, political legitimacy cannot be sustained through centralisation alone.
Strong federations require institutions that are nationally credible while remaining responsive to evolving regional aspirations.
The challenge therefore is not choosing between strong national institutions and stronger regional aspirations. It is designing governance arrangements capable of accommodating both.
That requires leadership, trust and institutional imagination.
In many respects, this is the central statecraft challenge facing modern federations: how to preserve national coordination without diminishing regional dignity, and how to strengthen regional participation without weakening national coherence.
Ultimately, Alberta, Scotland, Catalonia and Sarawak all point towards the same fundamental question confronting modern federations.
The issue is no longer simply who owns the resource.
The deeper question is whether national cohesion and regional dignity can coexist within a shared political framework.
The most successful federations of the 21st century will not be those that suppress regional aspirations, nor those that permit unchecked fragmentation.
They will be those capable of renewing legitimacy through dialogue, clarity and trust.
That is not merely a constitutional challenge. It is a test of nation-building itself.
Alberta today reminds us that even mature democracies and advanced federations must continuously renew political legitimacy.
The challenge confronting Canada, like Malaysia, is not merely how to manage resource wealth, but how to ensure that prosperity, representation and trust continue to reinforce one another within a shared national project.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa outlook holds firm despite challenging five months
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's stock exchange has faced a volatile first five months but analysts believe corporate earnings resilience and institutional buying will remain its key support pillars in the second half.
Despite mounting geopolitical and political risks, most research houses are standing by their year-end FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) targets.
Following the first-quarter earnings season, TA Securities, Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd, MBSB Research and CGS International Securities Malaysia Sdn Bhd maintained their FBM KLCI targets.
RHB Research and CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd, however, trimmed their forecasts slightly.
Kenanga Research, meanwhile, turned more optimistic and raised its target after stronger-than-expected corporate earnings.
The January-May period saw the benchmark swing sharply amid war in the Middle East, surging oil prices, record foreign fund outflows and growing concerns over domestic political developments.
So far this year, FBM KLCI gained 13.5 points, or 0.81 per cent, rising to 1,683.26 from 1,669.76, after the strong late 2025 and early 2026 runs.
In 2025, the index rose 2.89 per cent to close at 1,680.11 points from 1,632.87 points at the beginning of the year.
ANALYSTS STILL SEE UPSIDE
TA Securities maintained its end-2026 target of 1,760 points, based on 14.6 times 2027 earnings.
The firm said an earlier best-case scenario of 1,850 points has become increasingly unlikely following the collapse of indirect negotiations between Iran and the US, and renewed hostilities involving Israel and Hezbollah.
It also pointed to persistent foreign selling and domestic political uncertainties as factors clouding the outlook.
Nevertheless, TA Securities continues to favour a defensive investment strategy while selectively positioning for opportunities created by market volatility and long-term structural growth themes.
RHB Research lowered its end-2026 target to 1,750 points from 1,780 points following downward earnings revisions but maintained a constructive stance on Malaysian equities.
Its head of regional equity research Alexander Chia said investors should adopt a defensive strategy and a buy-on-weakness approach amid elevated geopolitical risks and uncertainty surrounding the upcoming Johor election.
CIMB Securities also turned slightly more cautious, trimming its target to 1,745 points from 1,754 points after lowering its valuation assumptions.
However, the firm upgraded the building materials, plantation, oil and gas, and technology sectors on improving earnings prospects.
Among the major research houses, Kenanga Research was the most optimistic.
Head of research Peter Kong raised the firm's year-end target to 1,770 points from 1,760 points, citing stronger-than-expected first-quarter earnings and continued value-unlocking initiatives among listed companies.
He said many companies had either met or exceeded earnings expectations, providing a significant buffer against external uncertainties and reinforcing confidence in the broader market outlook.
FIVE-MONTH PERFORMANCE
FBM KLCI showed resilience in the first five months of 2026, ending almost unchanged from the start of the year despite navigating a series of geopolitical shocks, foreign fund outflows and domestic political uncertainties.
The seemingly modest gain masks a highly volatile period for Malaysian equities.
The index surged to a year-to-date peak of 1,771.25 on Jan 27, fuelled by optimism over economic growth, governance reforms and renewed foreign interest in local equities.
January was the market's strongest month, with foreign investors returning as net buyers to the tune of RM1.05 billion after pulling out RM2 billion in December 2025.
Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a firmer ringgit and continued policy support from Bank Negara Malaysia helped lift sentiment.
However, the rally was short-lived.
By late February, rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and uncertainty surrounding US trade policies began weighing on risk appetite.
The situation escalated when military conflict involving the US and Israel against Iran triggered a sharp selloff across regional markets.
FBM KLCI fell from 1,716.61 on Feb 27 to 1,700.21 on March 2, representing a decline of 16.4 points or 0.96 per cent.
Selling pressure intensified in the following week, dragging the index to 1,674.17 on March 9, its lowest level during the conflict-driven correction.
March proved to be one of the most challenging months of the year as oil prices surged amid fears of supply disruptions in the Middle East.
Crude prices briefly climbed above US$118 per barrel, fuelling concerns over inflation and global economic growth.
Despite the turbulence, the market gradually stabilised as investors reassessed the economic impact of the conflict.
Supportive domestic policies, steady interest rates and selective buying in energy-related counters helped cushion the downside.
The recovery gained momentum in April, when easing geopolitical concerns and improving risk sentiment pushed the benchmark higher.
Foreign investors also returned as net buyers, bringing in RM241 million during the month.
FBM KLCI subsequently climbed to 1,758.85 on May 7, recovering more than 84 points, or five per cent, from its March trough.
Yet another challenge emerged in May.
Persistent foreign selling, weaker-than-expected corporate earnings and renewed Middle East tensions prompted investors to reduce risk exposure.
Foreign funds recorded net outflows of RM3.73 billion during the month, the largest monthly withdrawal this year, causing foreign ownership to fall to a record low of 18.6 per cent.
Concerns over the possibility of an early general election and MSCI index rebalancing activities further weighed on sentiment, sending the index lower through most of May and early June.
Nevertheless, the benchmark managed to rebound during the first week of June, rising for two consecutive sessions to close at 1,683.26 on June 5.
Market strategists said the index's ability to remain in positive territory year-to-date despite a war-driven selloff, record-low foreign shareholding and political uncertainties underscores the underlying strength of Malaysia's domestic institutional investor base.
Throughout 2026, local institutions consistently emerged as the market's stabilising force, absorbing foreign selling and providing liquidity support during periods of heightened volatility.
WINNERS AND LOSERS
Blue chip stocks delivered sharply mixed performances so far this year, with gains concentrated in selected industrial and utility counters while telecommunications, consumer and retail stocks lagged behind.
Among the FBM KLCI constituents, Petronas Chemicals Group Bhd (PetChem) emerged as the best-performing blue-chip stock, with its share price surging 55.6 per cent, adding RM15.52 billion in value.
The stock climbed RM1.94 to RM5.43 as at June 5, from RM3.49 at the start of the year.
This brought PetChem's market capitalisation to RM43.44 billion.
The Employees Provident Fund (EPF), the company's second-largest shareholder, also increased its exposure during the period.
The EPF's stake rose to 12.98 per cent, or 1.04 billion shares, from 12.42 per cent (993.2 million shares) over the five-month period.
Press Metal Aluminium Holdings Bhd ranked as the second-best performer among the blue chips, gaining RM2.05 or 29.1 per cent to RM9.10 from RM7.05 at the start of the year.
Meanwhile, YTL Power International Bhd saw its share price rose 81 sen or 24.3 per cent to RM4.14 from RM3.33.
On the losing side, Axiata Group Bhd recorded the steepest decline among the benchmark constituents. Its share price fell 61 sen, or 23.6 per cent, to RM1.97 from RM2.58 on Jan 2.
The decline erased RM5.6 billion in market value, with Axiata's market capitalisation shrinking to RM18.1 billion from RM23.7 billion over the same period.
Consumer goods giant Nestlé (Malaysia) Bhd was the second-biggest decliner, dropping RM20.22, or 17.7 per cent, to RM94.28 from RM114.50 at the start of the year.
Meanwhile, 99 Speed Mart Retail Holdings Bhd shed 47 sen, or 12.4 per cent, to RM3.31 from RM3.78, making it the third-worst performer among the blue-chip stocks.
[[nid:1457778]]
On the sectoral front, the technology emerged as the best-performing sector, surging 30.4 per cent to 72.32 points as of June 5, from 55.46 points at the start of the year.
The rally was driven by renewed investor interest in semiconductor-related and digital infrastructure stocks, supported by improving global demand prospects and optimism surrounding the artificial intelligence supply chain.
The index climbed to a 22-month high of 73.87 points on May 29 before easing over the following three trading sessions.
Industrial products and services followed closely, rising 15.44 per cent to 198.74 points from 172.16.
The property sector also recorded strong gains, climbing 14.14 per cent to 1,218.90 points from 1,067.96.
Healthcare rose 6.59 per cent to 1,627.83 points from 1,527.10, while utilities gained 4.48 per cent to 1,725.30 points from 1,651.40.
The plantation sector added 4.43 per cent to 8,671.54 points, supported by relatively stable commodity prices.
The energy sector advanced 3.84 per cent to 796.20 points from 766.77, while financial services posted a modest gain of 0.99 per cent to 19,784.31 points from 19,589.99.
However, several key sectors recorded declines over the same period.
The telecommunications and media sector was among the weakest performers, falling 12.18 per cent to 410.73 points from 467.68.
Consumer products and services declined 8.83 per cent to 487.87 points from 535.07.
Construction dropped 5.53 per cent to 282.67 points from 299.21, while real estate investment trusts fell 3.37 per cent to 920.76 points from 952.84, reflecting cautious investor positioning amid higher yield expectations.
Transportation and logistics was broadly flat but slightly negative, easing 1.00 per cent to 1,009.98 points from 1,020.23.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump: Netanyahu will have 'no choice' but to accept Iran deal
Article:
WASHINGTON: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu will have no choice but to accept whatever deal the US negotiates with Iran, US President Donald Trump told the Financial Times in an interview published on Sunday, because Trump "calls the shots".
"He won't have any choice," Trump was quoted as saying. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump walks out of NBC interview after challenge over election claims
Article:
WASHINGTON: President Donald Trump lasted through probing questions about his Iran war strategy and repeated weather disruptions during an interview with US broadcaster NBC – but angrily stormed out when confronted over his unfounded claims of election fraud.
"You're a one-sided, crooked network. Sorry. Let's call it quits because I've had enough. Thank you, darling. Have a good time," Trump told "Meet the Press" host Kristen Welker before standing up and walking out of the interview that aired Sunday.
The interview, filmed Friday, was conducted on a farm in Wisconsin, where Trump was addressing farmers. A tractor and hay bales served as a backdrop.
The interview was interrupted several times by weather and audio issues as torrential rain pounded the roof, making it difficult to continue the conversation.
"Is that wind or what?" Trump asked at one point.
"Hear that sound?" he said later. "That sound of thunder, lightning, rain."
Welker asked her technical crew if they should stop.
"No. People will understand – we're on a farm," the president replied.
Several questions followed about Iran, nuclear weapons and his campaign pledges to keep the United States out of foreign wars.
"First of all, I didn't guarantee no war. Why would I have built the strongest military in the world?" he said, then accusing Welker of being "a big liberal, a big progressive."
"No, I'm just a journalist," she countered.
Trump went on to say: "I don't like these endless wars. This is not an endless war. We've been doing this for three months."
The tone escalated again as the discussion turned to Trump's proposed taxpayer-funded program to compensate people he believes were victims of partisan prosecution under his predecessor, Joe Biden.
"I don't know what's going to happen with the weaponisation fund," he said. "I love the idea because people like you, the fake dirty press, the crooked press, people like stupid Biden ... they destroyed people. They sent people to jail who did nothing wrong."
"There is no evidence for what you're saying," Welker said.
Trump reiterated past false claims that the 2020 election was "rigged" and said last week's primary in California was, too.
Welker held her ground, asking the president to present evidence.
"You are either crooked or you're stupid," Trump finally shot back before leaving the camera's view while Welker attempted to keep him engaged.
On Sunday, Welker told viewers that she had spoken with Trump on Saturday about the weather disruptions and that he had agreed to give her another interview.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rescue, not cost, was priority in Jaslinda SAR mission, says Fire Dept
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Fire and Rescue Department has not done a full costing of the search and rescue  operation for  hiker Jaslinda Saludin, as such  assessments are not routinely conducted, said its director-general.
"The priority was  Jaslinda's rescue and the safety of the team, not the cost," Datuk Seri Nor Hisham Mohammad told the
New Straits Times
.
"We did not count the costs of food for the whole team, the pay for rescuers and others.
"If there is a need for financial assessment,  we will do it. For example, if there is a forest fire and a case study is required,  will we carry out the research and calculations. For individual cases, we do not usually compute the costs."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump tells Netanyahu: Don't retaliate against Iran
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Sunday he will call Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to urge him not to retaliate against Iranian missile strikes on Israel, news outlet Axios reported.
"I am going to call Bibi right now and tell him not to retaliate," Trump was quoted as saying by Axios journalist Barak Ravid in a phone interview, using the Israeli leader's nickname.
"Each of them had their fun. Israel had its strike and Iran had its strike. We don't need another one," Trump said, according to excerpts of which Ravid posted on X.
Sunday's missile barrage was Iran's first against Israel since an April ceasefire took hold in the Middle East war, and Trump voiced concern it could set back negotiations.
"The Iranian strikes didn't hurt anybody. Hopefully Israel is not going to retaliate. If Bibi strikes them back it's just gonna keep going like the last 47 years, or the last 3,000 years," the US president said.
"We are very close to a final deal with Iran. It is going to be a good deal. I don't want it to blow up because of what is happening now," he added.
Iran's powerful Revolutionary Guards called Sunday's attack a "warning" after Israel struck Beirut's southern suburbs earlier in the day, threatening wider strikes in the event of repeated aggression.
An April 8 ceasefire had halted major hostilities between Iran, Israel and the United States.
But efforts to turn the truce into a settlement have repeatedly stalled, and Sunday's launches were sure to further dampen hopes for a lasting peace as the war reached its 100th day.
In a separate interview with Fox News, Trump said the Iranian strikes were not going to help negotiations, which were "very close" to reaching an agreement.
"We're very close. I would say an agreement would be signed on Monday, Tuesday or Wednesday of this coming week. And now this takes place."
He urged Iran to "get back to the table and make a deal."
Trump also criticised Israel's strikes on Beirut on Sunday, saying he was "not happy about it."
The White House did not immediately respond to an AFP request for comment.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hiker's experience and skill kept her alive' - Mountain guide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Jaslinda Saludin survived nearly two weeks in the jungle by drawing on her experience, conserving energy, staying close to water sources and remaining calm, an expert tracker said.
Malim Gunung Malaysia Association president Muzafar Mohamad said Jaslinda's ultimate anchor,  however, was her immense will to live and see her family and friends.
"Her experience and motivation for her loved ones are what brought her out. She had that sense of responsibility that kept her highly motivated to find an exit.
"Though she entered the forest with a leg injury that slowed her pace, Jaslinda was otherwise in good health and knew exactly how to conserve her energy," he told the
New Straits Times
.
He said unlike some hikers who might panic, exhaust themselves and collapse, Jaslinda, 49, rationed her strength.
"She knew when to seek shade, when to rest, and when to move.
"Her hiking experience also proved life-saving when she was cut off from river water for three days.
"But she was never far from those streams. She packed a large 15-to-25-litre bag with essential gear that had helped her. She was only unlucky that she couldn't find the path back to the trail."
Muzafar added that Jaslinda understood navigation and recognised landmark signs like Bukit Berapit and Gunung Rayu, and knew her bearings.
"She needed to head west to reach safety at Pos Musuh.
"Her disappearance was a matter of bad luck rather than a lack of skill. She spent days circling the correct area but simply kept missing the connecting trail, sometimes arriving at spots just after search parties had left.
"Ultimately, her physical discipline and deep knowledge allowed her to stay hydrated and alive long enough to finally pinpoint the correct path and walk out of the jungle to safety."
Yesterday,  Jaslinda, who went missing while trekking in the Gunung Batu Putih area in Ipoh, Perak, was found safe.
She was among 14 hikers and two mountain guides who began the Trans Spencer Chapman expedition at 2 am on May 23 via the Pos Gedong–Gunung Bah Gading–Gunung Batu Putih–Kuala Woh route.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Best if Pas leaves PN, say Bersatu leaders
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:   Senior Bersatu leaders  believe it is better that  Pas leaves Perikatan Nasional   as soon as possible.
This, they said, is because PN needs to move quickly on state election preparations.
This comes on the heels of Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang saying that the party would   decide  on its  cooperation with Bersatu today.
One senior Bersatu leader, who spoke on condition of anonymity, said the party long  anticipated that  Pas would leave the coalition  and contest on its own or align itself with a new coalition involving Muafakat Nasional or Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin's faction.
"Pas has repeatedly said it wants to join hands with Umno and revive MN2.0. All of this has been done without discussion in PN and has been carried out behind closed doors.
"The sooner Pas  leaves PN the better, because PN wants to make preparations for the state elections and negotiate electoral pacts with other parties," the source added.
The source said Bersatu's grassroots also  believed Pas should leave, as it had  betrayed PN several times by conducting negotiations without discussing them within the coalition.
The source alleged that reports presented to the     Pas  syura council    during a meeting to discuss the party's cooperation with Bersatu on June 2, did not recommend a split. Instead, the council left the  decision to the Pas central working committee.
The source also criticised PN's  new chairman, Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Samsuri Mokhtar of Pas, saying the coalition had become   lethargic under his watch.
With  the Johor and Negri Sembilan elections looming, the source said Samsuri had yet to make a   statement or call a  sup-reme council meeting to discuss preparations.
"The PN chairman has gone missing. The Johor and Negri Sembilan  assemblies have   been dissolved. The PN chairman has made no statement and has not called a  supreme council meeting to discuss preparations and seat allocations."
The source said Samsuri was  unable  to act independently  as he  had to seek permission from   Hadi  and the party's syura council.
More critically, the source said, Hadi   could veto PN supreme council  decisions  despite not being a member.
Despite the internal tensions, the Bersatu source said the party was confident that PN still had the  support of voters.
The source added that Pas should focus  on strengthening PN rather than conducting backchannel negotiations to revive MN 2.0 or absorb Hamzah's party.
When asked to comment on these allegations, Pas deputy president Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man said they were untrue.
"I don't think I have to comment   because all of these views are untrue," Tuan Ibrahim said.
Earlier yesterday, Bersatu president Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin reiterated its commitment to maintain good relations with Pas  despite  tensions between the two parties.
